world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Game includes various field conditions affecting play appearance and player movement
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Field conditions alter gameplay physics and player animation responses

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Generic teams identified primarily through uniform colors matching real NFL franchises
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: No actual NFL teams or player names present due to licensing constraints; identity communicated through color scheme alone

environmental_storytelling.example: Each generic team assigned specific colors matching their real-world NFL counterpart (e.g., New York represented by Giants' blue and white)
Teams named after NFL cities to establish implicit geographic and cultural identity without licensed branding

environmental_storytelling.visual_manifestation: Above and behind quarterback viewpoint establishes immersive field perspective

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Viewed from above and behind position, indicating readiness and command of field
Password-based save system allows state persistence across sessions

notes: John Madden Football (1990) operates as a sports simulation rather than a narrative world-state game. Manifestation patterns are minimal and focused on team differentiation through color identity and geographic naming rather than faction conflict or danger signals. The game's world-state communication relies almost entirely on visual uniform colors and audible callouts. No political situations, danger zones, or faction rivalries are explicitly communicated beyond the competitive nature of American football itself. The paradigm is sports competition, not environmental storytelling or world danger communication.
